items.back: DNL is each step's deviation from one ideal LSB; INL is the accumulated (integral) deviation of the transfer curve from a straight line. DNL $< -1$ LSB means a code is never output - a missing code.

items.front: Describe the flash ADC and its trade-offs.
items.back: Uses $2^N - 1$ comparators against a resistor-ladder reference, resolving in a single clock - GS/s fast. Cost: comparator count and power grow as $2^N$, so it is limited to low resolution (and used as sub-stages elsewhere).

items.front: How does a SAR ADC work, and where does it fit?
items.back: A successive-approximation register runs a binary search: each clock it sets a bit, compares $V_{in}$ to an internal DAC, and keeps or clears it. N clocks per N-bit sample. Low power, moderate speed - the embedded workhorse.

items.prompt: A 12-bit ADC datasheet specifies SINAD = 68 dB at the input frequency of interest. What is its ENOB?
items.explanation: $\mathrm{ENOB} = (68 - 1.76)/6.02 = 66.24/6.02 \approx 11.0$ bits. The part delivers ~11 of its nominal 12 bits.

items.prompt: Which architecture is the typical choice for digitizing a radar/SDR IF that needs both high speed (>100 MS/s) and 12-16 bit resolution?
items.explanation: Pipelined ADCs chain low-resolution flash stages with inter-stage gain, achieving high sample rates with good resolution - the standard for radar/SDR front ends. SAR is too slow; dual-slope far too slow; pure flash too low-resolution.

items.prompt: A 10-bit ADC reports a DNL of -1.2 LSB at one code transition. What does this imply?
items.explanation: DNL more negative than -1 LSB means the step width is negative-going enough that an output code is skipped entirely - a missing code.

items.prompt: Which converter is best for a high-accuracy bench multimeter where speed is irrelevant?
items.explanation: Dual-slope integrating converters are slow but extremely linear and inherently reject mains-frequency noise when the integration window is chosen accordingly - the classic precision-meter architecture.

lessons.title: Sigma-Delta, DAC Architectures, Reconstruction & Jitter
lessons.content:
**Sigma-delta ($\Delta\Sigma$)** converters trade speed for resolution using three ideas. **Oversampling** at rate $OSR \times$ Nyquist spreads the same quantization noise over a wider band, lowering in-band noise by $10\log_{10}(OSR)$ dB (about 3 dB, half a bit, per octave). **Noise shaping** - a feedback loop with an integrator - pushes quantization noise *up* in frequency, out of the band of interest; an $L$-th-order loop gains an extra $(2L+1)\cdot 3$ dB per octave. **Decimation** then digitally low-pass filters and downsamples to Nyquist. The result: 1-bit-ish front end, 24-bit output, ideal for audio, weigh scales, and instrumentation - but high latency.

**DAC architectures** mirror the ADCs. **Binary-weighted**: one switched current/resistor per bit weighted $1,2,4,\dots$; simple but needs an impractically wide range of matched values at high N. **R-2R ladder**: only two resistor values repeated, far easier to match in silicon - the workhorse. **String (Kelvin)**: $2^N$ equal resistors tapped by a switch - inherently monotonic, used in low-resolution and as fine stages. **Sigma-delta DAC**: a 1-bit high-rate stream smoothed to an analog level.

Every DAC output is a staircase, so it needs a **reconstruction filter** - an analog low-pass that removes the images at multiples of $f_s$ and smooths the steps (the dual of the anti-alias filter). **Interpolation** (digital upsampling before the DAC) eases this filter, just as oversampling eases the anti-alias filter.

Finally, **aperture jitter** $t_j$ caps SNR independent of resolution: $\mathrm{SNR}_{jitter} = -20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in} t_j)$. At high input frequencies a few hundred femtoseconds of clock jitter can dominate - which is exactly why radar obsesses over clock purity and why analog/digital ground separation matters.

items.front: Name the three mechanisms a sigma-delta ADC uses to win resolution.
items.back: Oversampling (spreads quantization noise over a wider band), noise shaping (a feedback integrator pushes noise out of band), and decimation (digital LPF + downsample to Nyquist).

items.front: How much in-band SNR does plain oversampling (no shaping) buy per doubling of OSR?
items.back: About $10\log_{10}(2) \approx 3$ dB per octave - roughly half a bit. Noise shaping of order $L$ adds much more: an extra $(2L+1)\cdot 3$ dB per octave.

items.front: Why is the R-2R ladder preferred over a binary-weighted DAC?
items.back: It uses only two resistor values (R and 2R) repeated, which silicon can match precisely. Binary-weighting needs values spanning $2^N$ - impossible to match accurately at high resolution.

items.front: What is the reconstruction filter, and how does it relate to the anti-alias filter?
items.back: An analog low-pass after the DAC that removes the spectral images at multiples of $f_s$ and smooths the output staircase. It is the dual (mirror) of the input anti-alias filter.

items.front: Give the jitter-limited SNR relation and its key consequence.
items.back: $\mathrm{SNR}_{jitter} = -20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in} t_j)$. It depends on input frequency, not resolution - so at high $f_{in}$ a tiny clock jitter can cap SNR below the converter's ENOB.

items.prompt: A first-order ($L=1$) sigma-delta modulator is oversampled. Roughly how much in-band SNR is gained per octave of OSR increase?
items.explanation: Oversampling alone gives 3 dB/octave; first-order noise shaping adds $(2L+1)\cdot 3 = 3\times 3 = 9$ dB/octave total (about 1.5 bits per octave).

items.prompt: Which DAC architecture is inherently monotonic because it taps a chain of equal resistors with a single switch?
items.explanation: A string DAC connects $2^N$ equal resistors in series and selects a tap; because the tap only moves up the divider, the output can never decrease as the code increases - guaranteed monotonic.

items.prompt: An ADC digitizes a 100 MHz input. To keep jitter-limited SNR above 70 dB, what is the approximate maximum allowable aperture jitter?
items.explanation: $t_j = \dfrac{10^{-\mathrm{SNR}/20}}{2\pi f_{in}} = \dfrac{10^{-3.5}}{2\pi \times 10^8} \approx \dfrac{3.16\times10^{-4}}{6.28\times10^8} \approx 0.5$ ps. High input frequencies demand femtosecond-class clocks.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of digital interpolation (upsampling) before a DAC?
choices: To add quantization bits to the data
To push DAC images to higher frequencies, easing the analog reconstruction filter
To remove aperture jitter from the clock
To convert two's complement to offset binary
items.explanation: Raising the effective output rate moves the spectral images farther from baseband, so a gentler (cheaper, lower-order) analog reconstruction filter suffices - the output-side dual of input oversampling.

lessons.title: Clock Sources & Frequency Stability
lessons.content:
A synchronous system is only as good as its **clock source**. Several technologies trade cost against accuracy.

The **Pierce crystal oscillator** uses a quartz resonator — a mechanically vibrating crystal with an extremely high quality factor ($Q$ often $>10{,}000$) — as the frequency-selective element in an inverter-based loop. Quartz is piezoelectric, so its sharp mechanical resonance sets the frequency far more tightly than any LC tank. **Ceramic resonators** are cheaper, smaller, and start faster, but are looser (hundreds of ppm). **MEMS oscillators** use a micromachined silicon resonator plus an on-chip PLL/fractional divider; they are shock-robust and programmable but historically noisier. **RC oscillators** (the internal MCU clock) need no external parts but drift wildly with voltage and temperature — fine for a UART-free blink loop, useless for precise timing.

Accuracy is quoted in **parts per million**. A frequency error $\Delta f$ gives

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_0}\times 10^{6}.$$

A $\pm 20$ ppm crystal at $25\,\text{MHz}$ drifts up to $\pm 500\,\text{Hz}$. A plain crystal varies over temperature in a parabola; a **TCXO** adds temperature compensation ($\sim\pm 0.5$–$2$ ppm), and an **OCXO** holds the crystal in a tiny heated oven for $\sim\pm 0.01$ ppm — the precision reference your radar timing chain would demand. **Startup time** matters too: high-$Q$ crystals can take milliseconds to reach full amplitude, which firmware must wait out before trusting the clock.

items.front: Why does a quartz crystal set frequency far more precisely than an LC tank?
items.back: Quartz is a piezoelectric mechanical resonator with a very high quality factor $Q$ (often $>10{,}000$). The sharp, stable mechanical resonance gives tight frequency selectivity and low drift, far better than the modest $Q$ of an LC circuit.
items.hint: Think mechanical resonance and Q.

items.front: Name the four clock-source technologies in order of typical accuracy, worst to best.
items.back: RC oscillator (worst, percent-level drift) -> ceramic resonator (hundreds of ppm) -> quartz crystal (tens of ppm) -> TCXO/OCXO (sub-ppm, best).
items.hint: Internal RC at one end, oven-controlled at the other.

items.front: What is the formula relating a frequency error to ppm?
items.back: $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\times 10^{6}$. So a 500 Hz error on a 25 MHz clock is $\dfrac{500}{25\times10^{6}}\times10^{6} = 20$ ppm.
items.hint: Fractional error times one million.

items.front: What does a TCXO add over a bare crystal, and an OCXO over a TCXO?
items.back: A TCXO adds temperature compensation (correcting the crystal's parabolic temp curve) for ~$\pm0.5$-$2$ ppm. An OCXO holds the crystal in a heated oven at a fixed temperature for ~$\pm0.01$ ppm, at higher power and cost.
items.hint: Compensate vs. control the temperature.

items.front: Why must firmware wait after enabling a crystal oscillator before using its clock?
items.back: High-$Q$ crystals build amplitude slowly; startup can take milliseconds. Until the oscillation reaches full amplitude the clock is unreliable, so MCUs gate the system clock behind a 'clock ready/stable' flag.
items.hint: High Q means slow buildup.

items.front: What are the tradeoffs of a MEMS oscillator versus a quartz crystal?
items.back: MEMS uses a micromachined silicon resonator plus an on-chip PLL/divider: very shock- and vibration-robust, programmable frequency, small. Downsides are historically higher close-in phase noise/jitter and reliance on the internal PLL.
items.hint: Silicon resonator + PLL; rugged but noisier.

items.prompt: A datasheet specifies a $\pm25$ ppm crystal at a nominal $48\,\text{MHz}$. What is the worst-case absolute frequency error?
items.explanation: $\Delta f = \text{ppm}\times 10^{-6}\times f_0 = 25\times10^{-6}\times 48\times10^{6} = 1200\,\text{Hz} = \pm1.2\,\text{kHz}$.

items.prompt: Which clock source is the right choice for a precision frequency reference in a coherent radar timing chain?
choices: An undivided ring oscillator on the FPGA fabric
items.explanation: An OCXO holds the crystal at a controlled temperature for ~$\pm0.01$ ppm stability and low close-in phase noise — exactly what coherent radar needs. RC and ceramic are far too loose; a fabric ring oscillator is the worst, with large temperature/voltage drift and high jitter.

items.prompt: An MCU boots on its internal RC oscillator instead of the external crystal. The most likely consequence for a UART link is:
choices: Lower power consumption with no functional impact
Baud-rate error large enough to corrupt the serial link
Improved timing accuracy because RC has no startup delay
items.explanation: RC oscillators drift by percent-level over voltage and temperature. UART tolerates only a few percent total baud error, so an uncalibrated RC clock readily pushes the bit timing out of range and corrupts characters. This is a classic 'works on the bench, fails hot/cold' bug.

lessons.title: PLLs, Frequency Synthesis & Loop Bandwidth
lessons.content:
A **phase-locked loop** generates a high, programmable output frequency from a stable reference. Trace the loop in the diagram: the **phase-frequency detector (PFD)** compares the reference edge against the divided-down feedback edge and emits up/down pulses proportional to the phase error. A **charge pump** converts those pulses into current, which the **loop filter** (a low-pass RC network) integrates into a smooth control voltage. That voltage tunes the **VCO**, whose output is fed back through a **divider** ($\div N$) to the PFD.

In lock, the PFD sees zero phase error, so the divided VCO output equals the reference. With a reference predivider $\div M$ and feedback $\div N$:

$$f_{out}=\frac{N}{M}\,f_{ref}.$$

This is **frequency synthesis** — a $25\,\text{MHz}$ crystal becomes a $150\,\text{MHz}$ core clock with $N/M = 6$. **Fractional-N** PLLs dither $N$ to hit non-integer ratios for fine resolution.

The **loop bandwidth** (set by the loop filter) is the central tradeoff. A *wide* loop locks fast and tracks reference cleanly, suppressing VCO noise but passing reference noise; a *narrow* loop filters reference spurs and gives a cleaner far-out spectrum but locks slowly and lets the VCO's own noise through close to the carrier. **Lock time** scales inversely with loop bandwidth. You place the crossover where the reference and VCO phase-noise curves intersect to minimize total integrated noise.

A **DLL** is a cousin: instead of a VCO it uses a voltage-controlled *delay line* to align phase, so it cannot multiply frequency, only deskew or phase-shift — but it has no accumulating phase error and is unconditionally stable (first order).

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: A charge-pump PLL: PFD compares reference and divided feedback, charge pump + loop filter make a control voltage, VCO generates the output, and a $\div N$ divider closes the loop. In lock $f_{out}=N\,f_{ref}$.

items.front: List the five blocks of a charge-pump PLL and each one's job.
items.back: 1) PFD - measures phase/frequency error, emits up/down pulses. 2) Charge pump - turns pulses into current. 3) Loop filter - low-passes current into a smooth control voltage. 4) VCO - converts voltage to frequency. 5) Divider $\div N$ - scales output back for comparison.
items.hint: PFD, pump, filter, VCO, divider.

items.front: What is the output frequency of a PLL with reference predivider M and feedback divider N?
items.back: $f_{out}=\dfrac{N}{M}\,f_{ref}$. The PFD drives the divided feedback to equal the divided reference, so the VCO must run at $N/M$ times the reference.
items.hint: Feedback divider multiplies, reference divider divides.

items.front: What does a fractional-N PLL do that an integer-N PLL cannot?
items.back: It dithers/modulates the feedback divider between integer values (often with a delta-sigma modulator) so the *average* divide ratio is fractional, giving very fine output frequency resolution without needing a tiny reference frequency. The cost is added fractional spurs/noise.
items.hint: Average a changing N to get a fraction.

items.front: How does a DLL differ structurally from a PLL, and what can it not do?
items.back: A DLL replaces the VCO with a voltage-controlled delay line and aligns phase rather than frequency. It cannot multiply frequency (no oscillator), only deskew or phase-shift an existing clock. It has no accumulating phase error and is unconditionally (first-order) stable.
items.hint: Delay line, not oscillator.

items.front: Why is the loop filter the component that sets PLL loop bandwidth?
items.back: The loop filter integrates and low-passes the charge-pump current into the VCO control voltage. Its pole/zero locations set how fast the control voltage can respond, which is exactly the loop's bandwidth and the dominant factor in stability and lock time.
items.hint: It shapes the control-voltage response.

items.prompt: You need a $156.25\,\text{MHz}$ clock from a $25\,\text{MHz}$ reference. Using $f_{out}=\frac{N}{M}f_{ref}$, which $(N, M)$ works?
items.explanation: Need $N/M = 156.25/25 = 6.25 = 25/4$, so $N=25, M=4$ gives $\frac{25}{4}\times25=156.25\,\text{MHz}$. $6/1$ gives 150 MHz, $25/8$ gives $\approx78\,\text{MHz}$, and $125/25=5$ gives 125 MHz.

items.prompt: Choosing a NARROW PLL loop bandwidth (relative to a wide one) primarily results in:
choices: Faster lock and better suppression of VCO close-in phase noise
Slower lock, better filtering of reference spurs, but more VCO close-in noise passes through
Higher output frequency for the same divider settings
Elimination of the need for a loop filter
items.explanation: A narrow loop filters reference-side noise/spurs well and gives a clean far-from-carrier spectrum, but locks slowly and does little to suppress the VCO's own close-in phase noise (the loop can't correct what it can't track fast). Lock time scales inversely with loop bandwidth.

items.prompt: A design must phase-align (deskew) an incoming clock to internal logic but does NOT need a different frequency. The best choice is:
items.explanation: A DLL uses a voltage-controlled delay line to align phase without changing frequency, is unconditionally stable, and adds no VCO phase noise. A PLL would work but is overkill and adds VCO jitter; deskew at the same frequency is precisely the DLL's job.

lessons.title: Jitter, Phase Noise, Clock Distribution & FPGA Resources
lessons.content:
A clock's *timing imperfection* shows up two ways. In the **time domain** it is **jitter**: **period jitter** (variation of one cycle's length), **cycle-to-cycle jitter** (adjacent-period difference), and **long-term/accumulated jitter**. In the **frequency domain** the same imperfection is **phase noise** $\mathcal{L}(f)$, the power in skirts around the carrier, in dBc/Hz. The two are Fourier duals: integrating phase noise over a band yields RMS jitter.

Why an EE who built radars cares: clock jitter directly caps converter performance. For a sine input at $f_{in}$ sampled by an ADC with RMS aperture jitter $t_j$, the jitter-limited SNR is

$$\text{SNR}_{jitter}=-20\log_{10}\!\left(2\pi f_{in}\,t_j\right)\ \text{dB}.$$

At high IF this can dominate over quantization noise — capping the ENOB you computed in Module 11. In **radar**, oscillator phase noise sets the **Doppler/clutter floor**: close-in phase noise masks slow-moving targets near strong clutter, and pulse-to-pulse jitter blurs coherent integration. Phase coherence is everything for MTI/Doppler processing.

**Clock distribution** must deliver this clock with minimal **skew** (arrival-time difference between endpoints). Designs use balanced **clock trees** (H-trees) and matched buffers so every flip-flop sees the edge together; skew eats directly into the setup/hold budget from Module 8.

On an **FPGA**, you do not route clocks on ordinary fabric. Dedicated **clock-capable input pins** feed **global clock buffers** (low-skew spines) and **MMCM/PLL** blocks (the 'clock wizard') that synthesize, phase-shift, and deskew derived clocks. **Spread-spectrum clocking** deliberately dithers the frequency slightly to spread EMI energy — helpful for compliance, but it adds jitter you must not feed into a sampling clock.

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: A 25 MHz crystal feeds a PLL/MMCM that multiplies it (x6 -> 150 MHz); a global clock buffer drives a balanced H-tree so the clock reaches every flip-flop with minimal skew.

items.front: Distinguish period jitter, cycle-to-cycle jitter, and long-term jitter.
items.back: Period jitter: deviation of a single clock period from ideal. Cycle-to-cycle jitter: difference between two adjacent periods (rate of change). Long-term/accumulated jitter: phase error built up over many cycles relative to an ideal clock.
items.hint: One period, adjacent periods, many periods.

items.front: How are jitter (time domain) and phase noise (frequency domain) related?
items.back: They are Fourier duals of the same imperfection. Phase noise $\mathcal{L}(f)$ is power in skirts around the carrier (dBc/Hz); integrating it over a frequency band and converting yields the RMS jitter in that band.
items.hint: Integrate phase noise to get RMS jitter.

items.front: Give the jitter-limited SNR of an ADC and explain why high input frequency hurts.
items.back: $\text{SNR}_{jitter}=-20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in} t_j)$ dB, where $t_j$ is RMS aperture jitter. SNR falls as $f_{in}$ rises because a fixed timing error $t_j$ produces a larger voltage error on a faster-slewing signal ($\Delta V \approx t_j \cdot dV/dt$).
items.hint: Error voltage scales with slew rate, which scales with f_in.

items.front: Why does radar care intensely about oscillator phase noise?
items.back: Coherent (MTI/Doppler) processing relies on stable pulse-to-pulse phase. Close-in phase noise raises the clutter/Doppler floor, masking slow targets near strong returns, and jitter smears coherent integration — degrading detection and velocity accuracy.
items.hint: Phase coherence sets the Doppler/clutter floor.

items.front: What is clock skew and how do FPGAs minimize it?
items.back: Skew is the difference in clock arrival time between endpoints. FPGAs route clocks on dedicated low-skew global clock buffers/spines (fed by clock-capable pins) and balanced trees, not ordinary fabric routing, so all flip-flops see the edge nearly simultaneously.
items.hint: Arrival-time difference; use dedicated global buffers.

items.front: What is spread-spectrum clocking and why can it be a problem for sampling?
items.back: SSC deliberately dithers the clock frequency slightly to spread EMI energy over a band, lowering peak emissions for compliance. But the intentional frequency modulation is added jitter, so you must not use an SSC clock as an ADC/DAC sampling clock where timing purity sets SNR.
items.hint: Smears EMI peaks, but adds jitter.

items.prompt: An ADC samples a $70\,\text{MHz}$ IF with $0.5\,\text{ps}$ RMS aperture jitter. Approximately what jitter-limited SNR results? (Use $\text{SNR}=-20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in}t_j)$.)
items.explanation: $2\pi f_{in} t_j = 2\pi(70\times10^{6})(0.5\times10^{-12}) \approx 2.20\times10^{-4}$. $-20\log_{10}(2.20\times10^{-4}) \approx 73\,\text{dB}$. At higher IF the same 0.5 ps would cap SNR even lower, limiting ENOB.

items.prompt: On a Xilinx-style FPGA, the correct way to derive a 200 MHz processing clock from a 50 MHz board oscillator is to:
choices: Build a ring oscillator from LUTs and divide it on the fabric
Bring the oscillator in on a clock-capable pin and use an MMCM/PLL (clock wizard) onto global clock buffers
Route the 50 MHz clock through general fabric and gate it with an enable to multiply it
Use a combinational counter to AND the clock with itself
items.explanation: Clocks enter on dedicated clock-capable input pins, feed an MMCM/PLL to synthesize the derived frequency, and distribute on low-skew global clock buffers. Fabric ring oscillators are jittery and uncontrolled; you cannot 'gate to multiply,' and gating clocks on fabric wrecks skew/timing (Module 8's rule).

items.prompt: Two clock domains in an FPGA come from the SAME MMCM with related, phase-aligned outputs. Compared to two independent oscillators, this means:
choices: Crossing between them never needs any synchronizer
The clocks are synchronous/related, so timing can often be constrained and analyzed statically rather than treated as a true asynchronous crossing
Skew is irrelevant because they share an MMCM
Jitter is automatically zero on both outputs
items.explanation: Outputs of one MMCM are frequency- and phase-related, so the tool can analyze paths between them with static timing rather than as a metastability-prone asynchronous crossing. Truly independent oscillators are asynchronous and still require synchronizers/async FIFOs. Shared MMCM does not zero out skew or jitter.

lessons.title: What an HDL Is (and Isn't): Hardware, Not Software
lessons.content:
## HDL describes hardware, not a program

The single biggest mental reset for a software developer: **HDL describes structure, not steps**. When you write Verilog, you are drawing a *parallel* schematic in text — every statement at module scope is "alive" simultaneously, like wires soldered on a board. There is no program counter, no top-to-bottom execution. A C `for` loop iterates over time; an HDL construct *unrolls into hardware* that all exists at once.

Two HDLs dominate. **Verilog** (and its modern superset **SystemVerilog**) is C-flavored and is the default in commercial chip and FPGA work. **VHDL** is Ada-flavored, verbose, and strongly typed; it is entrenched in defense, aerospace, and radar shops, so reading literacy pays off **[->08]**. We focus on Verilog and read VHDL.

A design is a tree of **modules**. Each module has a name, a port list (its pins), and a body that connects sub-modules and logic:

```verilog
module and_gate(input a, input b, output y);
  assign y = a & b;   // continuous: y tracks a&b forever
endmodule
```

That `assign` is not "run once." It is a permanent connection — change `a` or `b` and `y` updates after a propagation delay, exactly like a real gate. The whole flow mirrors test-driven software you already know: **simulate, then synthesize**. Simulation runs your HDL as an event model to check behavior; synthesis maps the *synthesizable subset* onto real LUTs and flip-flops. Keeping those two worlds straight is the theme of this entire sub-topic.

items.front: How does HDL execution differ from a C program?
items.back: It does not execute step-by-step. HDL describes parallel hardware: every module-scope statement is concurrently 'live,' like wires on a board. There is no program counter.

items.front: What are the two dominant HDLs and where does each prevail?
items.back: Verilog/SystemVerilog: C-flavored, the commercial chip/FPGA default. VHDL: Ada-flavored, strongly typed, entrenched in defense/aerospace/radar.
items.hint: One is C-like, one is Ada-like.

items.front: What is a Verilog `module`?
items.back: The fundamental design unit: a named block with a port list (its pins) and a body that connects sub-modules and logic. Designs are trees of modules.

items.front: What does a continuous `assign` statement do over time?
items.back: It is a permanent connection, not a one-time run. The LHS continuously tracks the RHS expression, updating after propagation delay, exactly like a real gate.

items.front: What is the simulate-then-synthesize workflow?
items.back: Simulation runs the HDL as an event model to verify behavior; synthesis maps the synthesizable subset onto real LUTs/flip-flops. It mirrors test-driven development.
items.hint: Two separate tools, two purposes.

items.front: Why is 'an always block is a function call' a dangerous analogy?
items.back: Because it isn't a call. always blocks describe persistent hardware that reacts to events forever; treating HDL as sequential/procedural software leads to wrong designs.

items.prompt: What is the most accurate description of what Verilog code represents?
choices: A sequence of CPU instructions executed top-to-bottom
A structural description of parallel hardware
A scripting language for configuring a CPU
A purely mathematical proof of correctness
items.explanation: HDL describes hardware structure that all exists simultaneously; there is no program counter or sequential execution at module scope.

items.prompt: Why is VHDL literacy specifically valuable for this learner?
choices: It is faster to simulate than Verilog
It is the only synthesizable HDL
Defense, aerospace, and radar shops commonly use it
items.explanation: VHDL is entrenched in defense/aerospace/radar [->08], so reading it opens those doors even when you write Verilog day-to-day.

items.prompt: In `assign y = a & b;`, when does `y` change?
choices: Only when the module is explicitly called
Continuously, whenever a or b changes (after delay)
Only on the next clock edge
items.explanation: A continuous assignment is a permanent connection; y tracks a&b at all times, like a physical AND gate, with no clock involved.

lessons.title: Modules, Ports, and wire vs reg
lessons.content:
## Modules, ports, and `wire` vs `reg`

A module's interface is its **port list**: `input`, `output`, or `inout`, each optionally a vector (bus). Vectors use a `[msb:lsb]` range:

```verilog
module adder #(parameter W = 8)
  (input  [W-1:0] a, b,
   input          cin,
   output [W-1:0] sum,
   output         cout);
  assign {cout, sum} = a + b + cin;
endmodule
```

`{cout, sum}` is **concatenation** — here it captures the carry-out into the extra top bit. `#(parameter W=8)` makes the module *sized at instantiation*, the HDL answer to generics/templates.

Now the classic confusion: **`wire` vs `reg`**. These are not "combinational vs sequential" — they are about *who drives the signal*. A `wire` is driven continuously, by an `assign` or by a module output. A `reg` is a variable assigned *inside a procedural* (`always`/`initial`) block. A `reg` does **not** imply a hardware register; an `always @(*)` block assigning a `reg` synthesizes to pure combinational logic. The name is a historical wart.

The rule of thumb:
- Left side of `assign`, or a module output port left unconnected procedurally -> `wire`.
- Anything assigned inside an `always`/`initial` -> `reg`.

SystemVerilog fixes the wart with **`logic`**, a single 4-state type usable in both contexts; you simply stop worrying about `wire`/`reg` and write `logic`. Ports default to `wire`/`logic` on input; outputs you assign procedurally must be `reg`/`logic`. Vectors slice naturally: `bus[3:0]`, `bus[i]`, and the literal width syntax `4'b1010` (4 bits, binary) or `8'hFF` (8 bits, hex) keeps sizes explicit — vital, because mismatched widths silently zero-extend or truncate.

items.front: What declares a module port and how are buses sized?
items.back: `input`/`output`/`inout`, optionally a vector with a `[msb:lsb]` range, e.g. `input [7:0] a`. The range gives the bus width.

items.front: What does `#(parameter W=8)` do?
items.back: It declares a parameter so the module is sized at instantiation, the HDL equivalent of a generic/template. Override it per instance to reuse the module at any width.
items.hint: Think C++ template / generic.

items.front: What does the concatenation `{cout, sum} = a + b + cin;` accomplish?
items.back: It joins signals into one wider bus; here the carry-out lands in the extra top bit while the lower bits hold the sum, capturing overflow cleanly.

items.front: What truly distinguishes `wire` from `reg` in Verilog?
items.back: Who drives it, NOT comb vs seq. A wire is continuously driven (assign/module output); a reg is assigned inside a procedural always/initial block.
items.hint: It is about the driver, not about clocking.

items.front: Does declaring something `reg` create a hardware register?
items.back: No. A reg assigned in `always @(*)` synthesizes to pure combinational logic. 'reg' is a historical naming wart, not a flip-flop guarantee.

items.front: What does SystemVerilog `logic` solve?
items.back: It is a single 4-state type usable in both continuous and procedural contexts, so you stop choosing between wire and reg and just write logic.

items.front: Why are sized literals like `4'b1010` and `8'hFF` important?
items.back: They make bit widths explicit. Mismatched widths silently zero-extend or truncate, a quiet source of bugs; explicit sizing prevents it.

items.prompt: Which statement about `wire` vs `reg` is correct?
choices: reg always means a flip-flop in hardware
wire is for combinational, reg is for sequential logic
The distinction is which one is procedurally assigned
wire can only be 1 bit wide
items.explanation: The split is about how a signal is driven: continuous (wire) vs assigned inside a procedural block (reg). A reg in always @(*) is combinational.

items.prompt: A signal assigned inside an `always @(*)` block must be declared as:
items.explanation: Anything assigned in a procedural block must be reg (or SystemVerilog logic); wire is only for continuous assignment / port connections.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of `parameter` in a module header?
choices: To make the module sized/configurable at instantiation
To mark the module as simulation-only
items.explanation: Parameters (Verilog) / generics (VHDL) let one module be reused at different widths or constants, set when the module is instantiated.

lessons.title: Combinational vs Sequential: = , <= , and Why It Matters
lessons.content:
## Combinational vs sequential: `=`, `<=`, and the rules

This lesson is the heart of the sub-topic and the source of the field's #1 trap. There are two procedural block styles.

**Combinational** logic uses `always @(*)` (or a continuous `assign`). The `(*)` is an *automatic complete sensitivity list* — it re-evaluates whenever any input changes. Inside, use **blocking** assignments (`=`), which execute in order like software, so intermediate temporaries read back immediately:

```verilog
always @(*) begin          // pure combinational
  if (sel) y = a;
  else     y = b;          // every branch assigns y
end
```

**Sequential** logic uses `always @(posedge clk)` and must use **non-blocking** assignments (`<=`):

```verilog
always @(posedge clk) begin
  q1 <= d;                 // all RHS sampled, THEN
  q2 <= q1;                // all LHS updated together
end
```

**Why `<=` matters:** non-blocking assignments evaluate *all right-hand sides* using the values *at the clock edge*, then update *all left-hand sides* simultaneously. That is exactly what a bank of flip-flops does — every FF samples its input at the edge at once. The code above is a 2-stage shift register. Rewrite it with blocking `=` and `q2 = q1` reads the *already-updated* `q1`, collapsing two flip-flops into one: it may simulate "fine" yet synthesize wrong, or create a simulation race versus synthesis mismatch.

**The discipline, memorized:**
- Clocked (`posedge clk`) block -> **non-blocking `<=`**.
- Combinational (`always @(*)`) block -> **blocking `=`**.
- Never assign the same signal from two different `always` blocks.

This rule comes from Cliff Cummings' SNUG papers and is non-negotiable. Drill it until it is reflex; it is *the* HDL rust-knocker.

items.front: What does `always @(*)` mean and what assignment goes in it?
items.back: It is a combinational block with an automatic complete sensitivity list (re-evaluates on any input change). Use blocking `=` assignments inside.
items.hint: Combinational; the (*) builds the list for you.

items.front: What block and assignment operator model a flip-flop?
items.back: `always @(posedge clk)` with non-blocking `<=` assignments. This is the only correct way to infer edge-triggered registers.

items.front: WHY must clocked blocks use non-blocking `<=`?
items.back: Non-blocking samples ALL right-hand sides at the clock edge, then updates ALL left-hand sides together, exactly mirroring a bank of flip-flops sampling at once.
items.hint: Real flip-flops all sample simultaneously.

items.front: What happens if you use blocking `=` in `q1=d; q2=q1;` inside a clocked block?
items.back: q2 reads the already-updated q1, collapsing a 2-stage shift register into a single flip-flop, and can cause sim-vs-synthesis mismatch / races.

items.front: Why do blocking assignments behave 'like software' in a comb block?
items.back: They execute in order, so an intermediate temporary is read back immediately by later lines, which is what you want for combinational data flow.

items.front: State the memorized blocking/non-blocking discipline.
items.back: Clocked posedge block -> non-blocking <=. Combinational always @(*) -> blocking =. Never drive the same signal from two always blocks.
items.hint: Two lines plus one prohibition.

items.front: Why can the wrong `=`/`<=` choice be especially insidious?
items.back: It can simulate 'fine' while synthesizing to different hardware (or create a simulation race), so the bug hides until hardware behaves unexpectedly.

items.front: Whose practitioner notes codified the non-blocking rule?
items.back: Cliff Cummings' SNUG papers on non-blocking assignments; they are the definitive reference for the discipline.
items.hint: SNUG conference papers.

items.prompt: Inside `always @(posedge clk)`, which assignment operator is correct?
items.explanation: Clocked blocks model flip-flops; non-blocking <= samples all RHS at the edge then updates all LHS together, matching simultaneous FF behavior.

items.prompt: Why does `q1<=d; q2<=q1;` correctly build a 2-stage shift register?
choices: Because <= updates q1 before q2 reads it
Because both RHS use the OLD values, sampled at the edge, then update together
Because = would be even more correct here
items.explanation: Non-blocking evaluates q1 and q2's RHS using pre-edge values, then updates both, so q2 gets the OLD q1 and two real flip-flops are inferred.

items.prompt: Using blocking `=` for `q1=d; q2=q1;` in a clocked block typically yields:
choices: A single flip-flop, because q2 reads the new q1
items.explanation: Blocking executes in order, so q2 reads the already-updated q1, collapsing both stages into one register, often with sim/synth mismatch.

items.prompt: Which is the correct operator choice by block type?
choices: Blocking in clocked, non-blocking in combinational
Non-blocking in clocked, blocking in combinational
items.explanation: The discipline: non-blocking <= in posedge clk blocks, blocking = in always @(*) combinational blocks. This is the #1 HDL rust-knocker.

items.prompt: Mixing up `=` and `<=` is dangerous mainly because:
choices: It always produces a compile error caught immediately
It can simulate fine yet synthesize to different hardware
It only affects VHDL, not Verilog
items.explanation: The failure is silent: behavior may match in simulation but diverge in synthesized hardware (or create a race), hiding the bug until late.

lessons.title: FSMs, Inferred Latches, Testbenches, and SystemVerilog
lessons.content:
## FSMs, latches, testbenches, and SystemVerilog

**Inferred latches** are the second great trap. In a combinational `always @(*)`, if a signal is *not* assigned on every path, the synthesizer must "remember" its old value — it infers a transparent **latch**, which wrecks timing and is almost never intended. The fix: assign every output a **default first**, and complete every `if` with an `else` and every `case` with `default`.

```verilog
always @(*) begin
  y = 1'b0;          // default kills latch inference
  if (sel) y = a;    // no else needed now
end
```

The **three-block FSM template** (from Module 7) is the canonical sequential pattern:

```verilog
// 1) state register (clocked, non-blocking)
always @(posedge clk)
  if (rst) state <= IDLE;
  else     state <= next;
// 2) next-state logic (combinational, default + case)
always @(*) begin
  next = state;            // default avoids a latch
  case (state)
    IDLE: if (go) next = RUN;
    RUN:  next = IDLE;
  endcase
end
// 3) output logic (combinational)
assign busy = (state == RUN);
```

**Testbenches** are simulation-only and may use non-synthesizable constructs freely: `initial` blocks, `#10` delays, `$display`/`$monitor`, and self-checking `if (got !== exp) $error(...)`. Drive stimulus, compare against expected, and view waveforms in GTKWave. Never put `#delays` or `initial` for state in *synthesizable* RTL — that is the **synthesizable vs sim-only** line.

**SystemVerilog** makes intent explicit and lets the tool *enforce* it: `always_ff @(posedge clk)` flags accidental combinational use, `always_comb` auto-builds the sensitivity list *and warns on inferred latches*, and `logic` replaces `wire`/`reg`. Use them on every new design.

items.front: What is an inferred latch and why is it bad?
items.back: In a comb always @(*), if a signal isn't assigned on every path, the tool keeps its old value by inferring a transparent latch, wrecking timing; almost always unintended.
items.hint: Happens with incomplete if/case.

items.front: How do you prevent inferred latches?
items.back: Assign every output a default value first, and complete every `if` with `else` and every `case` with `default` so every path drives the signal.

items.front: Name the three blocks of the canonical HDL FSM template.
items.back: 1) clocked state register (state <= next), 2) combinational next-state logic (default + case), 3) combinational/registered output logic.
items.hint: Register, next-state, output.

items.front: Why is `next = state;` placed first in the next-state block?
items.back: It is a default assignment so that any unlisted case path still drives `next`, preventing an inferred latch on the next-state signal.

items.front: What constructs are legal in a testbench but NOT in synthesizable RTL?
items.back: `initial` blocks for state, `#delays`, `$display`/`$monitor`, and `$error`. They are simulation-only; using them as hardware state crosses the synth/sim line.

items.front: What makes a testbench 'self-checking'?
items.back: It compares the DUT output against an expected value and flags mismatches automatically, e.g. `if (got !== exp) $error(...)`, instead of relying on eyeballing waveforms.

items.front: What does SystemVerilog `always_comb` add over `always @(*)`?
items.back: It auto-builds the sensitivity list AND makes the tool warn/error on inferred latches and accidental sequential use, enforcing combinational intent.
items.hint: It enforces the intent the name states.

items.front: What does `always_ff @(posedge clk)` enforce?
items.back: It declares intent to model flip-flops; the tool flags any accidental combinational coding, catching mistakes the plain `always` keyword would silently allow.

items.prompt: An inferred latch most commonly results from:
choices: An incomplete if/case in a combinational block (a path leaves a signal unassigned)
items.explanation: When a comb always @(*) leaves a signal unassigned on some path, the synthesizer must remember its old value, inferring an unintended transparent latch.

items.prompt: Which technique most reliably avoids inferred latches?
choices: Assigning every signal a default value at the top of the comb block
Using blocking assignments in clocked blocks
items.explanation: A leading default (plus full else/default) guarantees every path drives the signal, so no latch is inferred. always_comb also warns when you slip.

items.prompt: In the three-block FSM template, the state register block should use:
items.explanation: The state register is sequential: clocked always @(posedge clk) with non-blocking <=. Next-state and output logic are the combinational blocks.

items.prompt: Which construct belongs ONLY in a testbench, never in synthesizable RTL?
items.explanation: `#delays`, $display/$monitor, and initial-for-state are simulation-only. Synthesizable RTL must avoid them; that is the synth-vs-sim boundary.

items.prompt: What is the chief advantage of SystemVerilog `always_comb` / `always_ff`?
choices: They let the tool enforce your combinational/sequential intent and catch inferred latches
They eliminate the need for clocks
They make wire and reg interchangeable in all contexts
items.explanation: always_comb auto-builds the sensitivity list and warns on inferred latches; always_ff flags accidental combinational coding. They encode and enforce intent.

lessons.title: The FPGA Fabric: What's Actually on the Die
lessons.content:
An **FPGA** (Field-Programmable Gate Array) is not a fixed chip — it is a sea of **configurable resources** wired together by a programmable interconnect, all defined at power-up by a loaded **bitstream**. Where a CPU executes instructions sequentially, an FPGA *becomes* your circuit: every block runs in parallel, every clock cycle, deterministically.

The building blocks you must know:

- **Configurable logic blocks (CLBs / logic cells / slices / ALMs).** The atom of the fabric is a **LUT + flip-flop**. A $k$-input **lookup table (LUT)** is a tiny $2^k\times 1$ SRAM that stores the truth table of *any* Boolean function of $k$ inputs (typically $k=4$ to $6$). Its output optionally feeds a **flip-flop** so the cell can be combinational or registered. This is *LUT-based* logic, not gate-based — synthesis maps your design into LUT contents.
- **Block RAM (BRAM).** Dedicated dual-port SRAM blocks (tens of kbits each) for buffers, FIFOs, and sample memory — far denser than building RAM out of flip-flops.
- **DSP slices.** Hardened **multiply-accumulate (MAC)** units (e.g., $18\times 18$ or $27\times 18$ multipliers with an accumulator). These are the workhorses for filters and FFTs.
- **Clock management (PLL/MMCM).** Synthesize, phase-shift, and deskew clocks from a board crystal.
- **Configurable I/O.** Banked I/O supporting many standards (LVCMOS, LVDS, etc.) with per-pin drive and termination.

Knowing the fabric is knowing your budget: every design is a placement of LUTs, FFs, BRAMs, and DSPs onto fixed silicon.

items.front: What is the fundamental logic element of an FPGA fabric, and what are its two parts?
items.back: The configurable logic cell, built from a **LUT + flip-flop**. The $k$-input lookup table (a $2^k\times1$ SRAM) computes any Boolean function of $k$ inputs; the optional flip-flop registers the output, making the cell combinational or sequential. Thousands of these plus a programmable interconnect form the fabric.
items.hint: Lookup table feeds an optional register.

items.front: How does a LUT implement an arbitrary logic function?
items.back: A $k$-input LUT is a $2^k$-entry SRAM addressed by the $k$ inputs; each entry holds the function's output for that input combination — literally the truth table. The bitstream loads the SRAM contents, so the same physical LUT can be AND, XOR, a 3-input majority, etc. This is why FPGA logic is 'LUT-based,' not fixed gates.
items.hint: It stores the truth table in SRAM addressed by the inputs.

items.front: What are DSP slices and why do they exist as hardened blocks?
items.back: DSP slices are dedicated, hardened **multiply-accumulate (MAC)** units (e.g., an $18\times18$ or $27\times18$ multiplier with a wide accumulator and pre-adder). Building a fast multiplier out of LUTs is slow and area-hungry, so vendors harden them — giving high clock rates and low power for the multiply-heavy math (FIR, FFT, correlation) that dominates DSP.
items.hint: Hardened multipliers + accumulators for the MAC math.

items.front: What is block RAM (BRAM) and when do you use it instead of flip-flops or distributed RAM?
items.back: BRAM is dedicated dual-port SRAM (typically tens of kbits per block) built into the fabric. Use it for anything sizeable: sample buffers, line/frame stores, FIFOs, coefficient tables, and CDC async FIFOs. Building the same storage from flip-flops or LUT-based 'distributed RAM' wastes huge fabric resources and won't reach the density or speed.
items.hint: Dedicated dual-port SRAM for buffers and FIFOs.

items.prompt: A 6-input LUT can implement how many distinct entries (and thus any function of 6 inputs)?
items.explanation: A $k$-input LUT is a $2^k\times1$ SRAM, so a 6-LUT has $2^6=64$ entries — the full truth table of any 6-input Boolean function. Picking $k=6$ balances logic density against the SRAM cost of $2^k$ bits per LUT.

items.prompt: Which on-chip resource is the right choice to buffer a 16k-sample block of ADC data inside the FPGA?
items.explanation: BRAM is dedicated dual-port SRAM sized exactly for buffers and FIFOs. Sixteen thousand samples in flip-flops would consume an enormous share of the fabric; DSP slices do arithmetic, not bulk storage; I/O is for pins.

items.prompt: What configures an FPGA's logic the moment it powers up?
choices: A bitstream loaded into the fabric's configuration SRAM
A compiled C program in flash executed by a CPU
Permanent metal mask layers set at the foundry
items.explanation: SRAM-based FPGAs hold their LUT contents, routing, and block settings in configuration SRAM, loaded from a bitstream at power-up (from flash or JTAG). It's volatile — remove power and the configuration is gone, which is why a config flash sits next to most FPGAs.

items.prompt: The clearest architectural difference between an FPGA and a CPU is that the FPGA:
choices: Physically instantiates spatial, parallel hardware that all runs every clock, rather than time-multiplexing one datapath across an instruction stream
Runs software faster because it has a higher clock frequency
Stores its program in DRAM instead of cache
Can only implement combinational logic, never sequential
items.explanation: An FPGA spends area to gain parallelism: you lay down as many MACs, FIFOs, and state machines as fit, and they all operate concurrently and deterministically each cycle. A CPU reuses one datapath sequentially. FPGAs usually clock *slower* than CPUs but win on throughput-per-clock and latency determinism.

items.front: A logic cell: LUT + flip-flop
items.back: A k-input LUT (an SRAM truth table) computes any Boolean function of its inputs; an output mux selects either the raw combinational LUT output or a registered version from the flip-flop (clocked by clk). Thousands of these cells plus a programmable interconnect make up the FPGA fabric.

lessons.title: The Implementation Flow: HDL to Bitstream
lessons.content:
Getting your HDL onto silicon is a pipeline of distinct tool stages. Treat it like a compiler-and-linker chain for hardware.

1. **Design entry (HDL).** Write synthesizable Verilog/VHDL (RTL) plus the IP you instantiate.
2. **Simulation.** Before touching the fabric, verify behavior with a **testbench** (Icarus/Verilator/vendor sim). Catching bugs here is orders of magnitude cheaper than on hardware.
3. **Synthesis.** The tool elaborates RTL and maps it to fabric primitives — LUTs, FFs, BRAM, DSP — and does logic optimization (the K-map/minimization work, automated). Output: a technology-mapped netlist.
4. **Place & route (P&R, 'implementation').** Assign each primitive to a physical site (place) and connect them through the routing fabric (route). This is the hard, slow, NP-ish step; routing congestion and physical distance set your achievable speed.
5. **Static timing analysis (STA).** Against your **timing constraints**, the tool checks every path's setup and hold using post-route delays and reports **slack**. This ties straight back to the setup/hold and $f_{max}$ math from the timing module — now with real numbers.
6. **Bitstream generation.** Serialize the placed-and-routed configuration into the binary the chip loads.
7. **Configuration.** Download the bitstream to the board (JTAG for bring-up, SPI flash for boot).

Along the way you read two report families constantly: the **utilization report** (how much LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP you used) and the **timing report** (did every path close?). Both gate whether the design is shippable.

items.front: List the FPGA implementation flow in order, from HDL to a configured board.
items.back: HDL design entry, then simulation, then synthesis (map RTL to LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP), then place & route, then static timing analysis (STA), then bitstream generation, then configuration (download to the board). Reports of utilization and timing are read throughout.
items.hint: Entry, sim, synth, P&R, STA, bitstream, config.

items.front: What does synthesis actually produce, and what does it optimize?
items.back: Synthesis elaborates RTL and maps it to fabric primitives (LUTs, flip-flops, BRAM, DSP slices), producing a technology-mapped netlist. It performs logic optimization automatically — the Boolean minimization and state-encoding work you'd do by hand with K-maps — and infers blocks like RAMs and multipliers from your coding patterns.
items.hint: RTL becomes a netlist of fabric primitives, with logic minimized.

items.front: What happens during place & route (P&R), and why is it the slow, hard step?
items.back: **Place** assigns each netlist primitive to a specific physical site on the die; **route** connects them through the fixed programmable interconnect. It's a large combinatorial optimization (NP-hard in general): the placer must minimize wire length and congestion while the router must find legal paths that also meet timing. Physical distance and routing delay ultimately cap your clock speed.
items.hint: Assign primitives to sites and wire them up — a huge optimization problem.

items.front: Why simulate before synthesizing, given you'll test on hardware anyway?
items.back: Simulation with a testbench gives full observability and fast iteration: you see every signal, can force inputs, and catch logic bugs in seconds. A full synth/P&R/bitstream cycle can take minutes to hours, and on-board debug has limited visibility (you only see what you route to a probe/ILA). Fixing a bug in sim is orders of magnitude cheaper than after implementation.
items.hint: Full observability and fast iteration vs slow builds and limited on-chip visibility.

items.prompt: In which stage are post-route, real interconnect delays used to verify that every path meets setup and hold?
items.explanation: STA runs after place & route, when actual cell and routing delays are known, and checks every timing path against the constraints, reporting slack. Behavioral simulation has no real delays; synthesis only estimates; bitstream generation just serializes the result.

items.prompt: Your design synthesizes and places fine but the timing report shows several paths failing. The most direct interpretation is:
choices: Those paths have negative slack — they can't meet the constraint at the requested clock, so the design is not guaranteed to work at speed
The design used too many LUTs
items.explanation: Failing timing paths have negative slack: required time minus arrival time is negative, meaning data doesn't settle before the capturing edge. The design may work intermittently or not at all at that clock. Fixes: pipeline the path, restructure logic, relax the clock, or fix constraints.

items.prompt: What is the role of a bitstream file in the flow?
choices: It is the serialized binary that loads LUT contents, routing, and block settings into the FPGA's configuration memory
It is the human-readable HDL source for the design
It is the timing report summarizing slack on each path
It is the testbench stimulus used in simulation
items.explanation: The bitstream encodes the entire placed-and-routed configuration — every LUT truth table, routing switch, and block setting. Loading it (via JTAG or from a config flash) is what turns the generic fabric into your specific circuit.

items.prompt: Two reports you consult on essentially every build are:
choices: The utilization report and the timing report
The power-on log and the crash dump
items.explanation: Utilization tells you how much LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP you've consumed (are you about to overflow the part?), and timing tells you whether every path closes (will it run at the target clock?). Together they decide whether the design fits and runs.

items.front: FPGA implementation flow
items.back: The flow runs HDL entry, then simulation against a testbench, then synthesis, then place & route, then static timing analysis (STA), then bitstream generation, then configuring the board. STA reports slack; negative slack fails timing and feeds back to fixing the RTL or the constraints.

lessons.title: Constraints, Slack, Utilization, and IP
lessons.content:
Two text artifacts and one library decide whether a design is real: **constraints**, **reports**, and **IP**.

**Timing constraints (.xdc for Vivado, .sdc for Quartus/SDC tools).** STA has no idea how fast you intend to run unless you tell it. At minimum you `create_clock` with a period; you also specify **input/output delays** (when external data is valid relative to a clock at the pins) and any false/multicycle paths. Garbage constraints give garbage timing sign-off — a design that 'meets timing' against no clock means nothing.

**Slack.** For each path, $\text{slack} = \text{required time} - \text{arrival time}$. **Positive slack** = margin (good). **Negative slack** = a violation. **Worst Negative Slack (WNS)** is the headline number; **Total Negative Slack (TNS)** sums all violations. Setup slack scales with the clock period; **hold slack is frequency-independent** — you can't slow the clock to fix a hold violation (a direct callback to the timing module).

**Utilization.** The report tallies LUTs, FFs, BRAM, and DSP used vs available. Hit ~80–90% and routing congestion spikes, P&R slows, and timing gets harder — leave headroom.

**Pin / physical constraints** map HDL top-level ports to physical package pins (`set_property PACKAGE_PIN`) and I/O standards — buttons, LEDs, PMOD, the clock input. Wrong pin assignment is the classic 'LED won't blink' bug.

**IP: hard vs soft.** **Hard IP** is fixed silicon (PCIe transceivers, DDR controllers, the PLL/MMCM, DSP) — fast and power-efficient, but fixed. **Soft IP** is synthesized into the fabric from HDL — flexible, costs LUTs. A **soft CPU** (RISC-V, MicroBlaze, Nios) is soft IP: a whole processor built in the fabric, letting you run C alongside custom accelerators.

items.front: What is a timing constraints file and what is the single most important thing it must declare?
items.back: It's a text file (.xdc for Vivado, .sdc for Quartus) telling STA the design's timing intent. The essential entry is the **clock**: `create_clock` with a period (and waveform). Without it STA has nothing to check against — 'meeting timing' against no clock is meaningless. It also sets input/output delays and false/multicycle paths.
items.hint: Defines clocks and I/O timing for STA; clock definition is mandatory.

items.front: Define slack, WNS, and TNS.
items.back: **Slack** = required time minus arrival time for a path; positive is margin, negative is a violation. **WNS** (Worst Negative Slack) is the single most-violating path's slack — the headline. **TNS** (Total Negative Slack) sums all negative slacks, indicating how many paths fail and how badly. You sign off timing when WNS $\ge 0$.
items.hint: Slack = required - arrival; WNS = worst path; TNS = sum of all violations.

items.front: Contrast hard IP and soft IP, with examples.
items.back: **Hard IP** is dedicated silicon: PCIe/SerDes transceivers, DDR memory controllers, PLL/MMCM, DSP slices — fast, power-efficient, but fixed in capability and location. **Soft IP** is HDL synthesized into the fabric (UARTs, AXI interconnect, FIR cores, a soft CPU) — fully flexible and portable across parts, but it consumes LUTs/FFs and runs at fabric speeds.
items.hint: Hard = fixed silicon block; soft = synthesized into LUTs.

items.front: What is a soft-core CPU, and why drop one into an FPGA?
items.back: A soft CPU (RISC-V, Xilinx MicroBlaze, Intel Nios) is a full processor described in HDL and synthesized into the fabric. You use it for control-plane and housekeeping tasks (config, slow protocols, decision logic) written in C, while the fabric does the heavy parallel DSP. It gives software flexibility without an external MCU, and RISC-V makes it open and license-friendly.
items.hint: A processor built from fabric logic; runs C for control while hardware does DSP.

items.prompt: STA reports a hold-time violation. Which action can fix it?
choices: Add delay on the short path or fix clock skew/routing — slowing the clock will NOT help
items.explanation: Hold violations are frequency-independent: $t_{cq}+t_{pd,min} \ge t_h + \text{skew}$ has no clock period in it. Slowing the clock fixes setup, never hold. You fix hold by adding delay to the too-fast path or correcting skew — usually the tools do it automatically by inserting routing.

items.prompt: A path has required time 8.0 ns and arrival time 9.3 ns. Its slack is:
items.explanation: Slack = required - arrival = 8.0 - 9.3 = -1.3 ns. Negative slack means data arrives after it was required (too late for the capturing edge): a setup violation. Pipeline the path, restructure logic, or relax the clock.

items.prompt: Your HDL is correct in simulation but the on-board LED never lights. The most likely cause is:
choices: A wrong or missing pin/physical constraint mapping the top-level port to the LED's package pin (or wrong I/O standard)
The synthesis tool minimized the LED logic away on purpose
The clock constraint period is too long
items.explanation: Simulation doesn't know about physical pins; only the constraints file maps HDL ports to package pins and I/O standards. A wrong PACKAGE_PIN, missing constraint, or incorrect I/O standard/voltage is the classic reason hardware behavior diverges from a perfect simulation.

items.prompt: Pushing fabric utilization toward 90% typically causes:
choices: Routing congestion that slows place & route and makes timing closure harder
Lower power and faster timing automatically
items.explanation: As the device fills, the router has fewer free tracks and must take longer detours, increasing delay and congestion. P&R runtime balloons and timing closure becomes fragile. Good practice is to leave headroom (often target well under ~80% for timing-critical designs).

items.front: Why are PLL/MMCM blocks usually hard IP rather than soft?
items.back: Clock generation needs analog/mixed-signal precision (voltage-controlled oscillator, phase detector, low jitter) that can't be built from LUTs. So vendors harden the PLL/MMCM as dedicated silicon you configure via a clocking wizard. It multiplies/divides the board crystal, phase-shifts, and deskews clocks — feeding the global clock network with low jitter.
items.hint: Clock synthesis is analog/mixed-signal; LUTs can't do it, so it's hardened.

lessons.title: FPGA vs MCU/ASIC/GPU, Ecosystems, and Why Radar/SDR Lives on FPGAs
lessons.content:
**Choosing the right silicon.** Four contenders, four sweet spots:

- **MCU:** cheapest, lowest power, easiest (C firmware), but a single sequential core — fine for control and modest sampling, poor for sustained high-rate DSP. **[->04]**
- **GPU:** thousands of cores, huge throughput on batched/floating-point workloads, but high power and **high, variable latency** (batches, memory hierarchy, OS) — great offline, awkward for hard-real-time streaming.
- **ASIC:** ultimate performance/watt and unit cost at volume, but enormous **NRE** (mask/design cost) and zero flexibility once taped out — only sane at very high volume.
- **FPGA:** reconfigurable parallel hardware with **deterministic, low latency** and line-rate throughput, no per-design mask cost, reprogrammable in the field. Wins for low-to-mid volume, evolving standards, and hard-real-time streaming DSP.

**Vendor ecosystems.** AMD/Xilinx → **Vivado** (Artix/Kintex/Zynq; Arty, Basys 3 boards). Intel/Altera → **Quartus** (Cyclone/Arria/Stratix). Lattice and small parts → the **open-source flow**: **Yosys** (synthesis) + **nextpnr** (P&R) + project IceStorm/Trellis (iCE40/ECP5; iCEBreaker, ULX3S). Open tools mean no license wall and scriptable, reproducible builds.

**Why FPGAs dominate radar/SDR.** Modern receivers digitize at hundreds of MS/s to GS/s, producing a firehose no single CPU can keep up with at low latency. FPGAs map the math spatially: arrays of **DSP-slice MACs** compute **FIR filtering**, **pulse compression** (matched filtering / correlation), **FFTs**, and **digital beamforming** every clock, in parallel, at line rate with deterministic latency. You instantiate, say, 128 taps as 128 MACs that all fire each cycle — the literal building block of a radar signal chain. That parallel-MAC-at-line-rate property, plus reconfigurability for evolving waveforms, is exactly why FPGAs (often alongside a soft or hard CPU on a Zynq-class device) are the centerpiece of contemporary radar and SDR — and why the skill is in demand. **[->08]**

items.front: When does an FPGA beat an MCU, and when does the MCU win?
items.back: FPGA wins when you need sustained high-throughput parallel processing with deterministic low latency (streaming DSP, line-rate filtering, custom protocols). The MCU wins for cost, power, and developer-speed on control-plane and modest workloads — sequential C is far easier than HDL. Many systems use both: MCU/soft-CPU for control, fabric for the heavy parallel math.
items.hint: FPGA = parallel/deterministic/high-rate; MCU = cheap/low-power/easy/sequential.

items.front: Compare FPGA vs ASIC on the axes that actually drive the decision.
items.back: ASIC gives the best performance-per-watt and lowest unit cost — but only after huge **NRE** (mask and design cost) and with **zero post-tapeout flexibility**. FPGA has higher per-unit cost and power but **near-zero NRE**, field reprogrammability, and fast iteration. ASIC pays off only at very high volume or extreme perf/power needs; FPGA wins for low-to-mid volume and evolving requirements.
items.hint: ASIC: low unit cost/high NRE/fixed. FPGA: higher unit cost/no NRE/reprogrammable.

items.front: Why is an FPGA often preferred over a GPU for real-time radar/SDR streaming, despite the GPU's raw throughput?
items.back: GPUs excel at batched, high-throughput floating-point but carry high and variable latency (batching, deep memory hierarchy, host/OS scheduling) and high power. Radar/SDR needs deterministic, low, fixed latency on a continuous sample stream at line rate. The FPGA processes each sample spatially every clock with predictable timing, making it the better hard-real-time streaming engine.
items.hint: GPU = high but variable latency; FPGA = deterministic low latency at line rate.

items.front: Name the three main FPGA toolchains/ecosystems and the families they target.
items.back: AMD/Xilinx → **Vivado** (Artix/Kintex/Zynq; Arty, Basys 3). Intel/Altera → **Quartus** (Cyclone/Arria/Stratix). Open-source → **Yosys + nextpnr** (with IceStorm/Trellis) for Lattice iCE40/ECP5 (iCEBreaker, ULX3S) and some Gowin parts. Open tools are license-free, scriptable, and reproducible.
items.hint: Vivado (Xilinx), Quartus (Intel), Yosys/nextpnr (Lattice, open).

items.prompt: A startup needs 2,000 units of an evolving SDR product with hard-real-time, line-rate filtering and must ship in months. Best choice:
choices: FPGA — parallel, deterministic, reprogrammable, near-zero NRE, fast iteration
items.explanation: Low-to-mid volume (2k units) and evolving requirements rule out the ASIC's huge NRE and fixed function. Hard-real-time line-rate DSP rules out the MCU (too sequential) and GPU (latency/power). The FPGA's reprogrammable parallel fabric with deterministic latency fits all three constraints.

items.prompt: The core reason FPGAs map radar signal processing so well is that they can:
choices: Instantiate many DSP-slice MACs that all compute in parallel every clock, sustaining FIR/pulse-compression/FFT/beamforming math at line rate with deterministic latency
Run a faster clock than any CPU or GPU
Store the entire radar return in a single flip-flop
Execute C code more efficiently than an MCU
items.explanation: Radar DSP is multiply-accumulate-heavy (filters, correlation, transforms, weighted sums for beamforming). An FPGA lays those MACs down spatially so all taps/channels compute concurrently each cycle at the ADC's sample rate, with fixed latency — exactly what hard-real-time radar needs, and what a sequential core cannot match at low latency.

items.prompt: Pulse compression in a radar receiver is implemented in the FPGA primarily as:
choices: A matched filter / correlation — a long FIR (MAC chain) against the transmitted waveform, often via FFT-multiply-IFFT
A single DSP multiply with no accumulation
A soft-CPU C loop running once per pulse
A look-up in block RAM with no arithmetic
items.explanation: Pulse compression correlates the return against the known transmit waveform (matched filtering), which is a long FIR/MAC operation (time-domain) or an FFT-multiply-IFFT (frequency-domain) — both MAC-dominated. The FPGA's DSP slices and BRAM make this feasible at the incoming sample rate with deterministic latency.

items.prompt: Why does a 128-tap FIR filter often run comfortably at line rate on an FPGA but stress a single CPU core?
choices: The FPGA can place 128 MACs that all execute every clock in parallel, while the CPU must execute the 128 multiply-accumulates sequentially per sample
The FPGA clock is 128 times faster than the CPU
FIR filters cannot run on a CPU at all
The CPU lacks any multiplier hardware
items.explanation: A direct-form FIR needs N MACs per output sample. The FPGA instantiates N DSP slices that fire concurrently each clock, producing one output per cycle. A CPU core does those N MACs in sequence (or a few via SIMD), so per-sample throughput drops as N grows — the classic spatial-vs-temporal trade-off.

items.front: On a Zynq-class device, how do the FPGA fabric and an on-chip CPU divide the radar/SDR workload?
items.back: The hardened CPU (e.g., ARM, or a RISC-V/MicroBlaze soft core) handles the control plane: configuration, mode/PRI sequencing, slow protocols, networking, and decision logic in C/Linux. The fabric handles the data plane: line-rate FIR, pulse compression, FFT, and beamforming in parallel DSP slices. They communicate over an on-chip bus (AXI), pairing software flexibility with hardware throughput.
items.hint: CPU = control plane (C); fabric = data plane (parallel DSP); linked by AXI.
